title: Fitting shrink-film secondary glazing to single-glazed windows
subtitle: A temporary, transparent film system that can halve heat loss through single-glazed windows
excerpt: Shrink-film secondary glazing is a transparent polyester film stretched across a double-sided tape frame on the window architrave and then heat-shrunk taut with a hairdryer. It traps a still-air layer in front of the glass, roughly halving heat loss through the window. Cost is around a modest cost.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust secondary glazing guidance for heat loss comparisons. Historic England advice on temporary secondary glazing for listed buildings and conservation areas. BRE Good Practice Guide 262 on secondary glazing options. Manufacturer product data for window insulation kit air-gap performance.

glossaryTerms.definition: Rate of heat transfer through a building element, W/m2K. Lower is better. Single glazing is around 5.0; with shrink film it falls to roughly 2.5.

content.text: Shrink-film secondary glazing is a polyester film kit applied to the inside of a window frame for the heating season. The film creates an air gap of around 15-20 mm in front of the glass. This still-air layer roughly halves the

of a single-glazed window from about 5.0 to 2.5 W/m2K. In a Victorian terrace with 14 single-glazed sash windows, fitting film to all of them saves around 2,500-3,500 kWh/year on a gas-heated house, worth a modest cost at 6p/kWh (2025/26 cap rates). Materials cost around a modest cost for all 14 windows.
Window insulation film kit (sold in rolls, typically 1.5 m wide, or as pre-cut single-window sets)
Double-sided tape (the kit tape is optimised to release cleanly at season end; don't substitute standard carpet tape)
Hairdryer on its highest heat setting for shrinking
Cloth for cleaning the window frame before taping
Clean the window frame or architrave with a damp cloth and let it dry completely. The tape must bond to clean, dry wood or paint.
Apply the double-sided tape to the architrave or stop bead, forming a continuous rectangle. Press it firmly and peel the backing off the outer face.

Cut the film 50 mm larger than the taped rectangle in each direction. Press the film onto the tape at the top of the window, stretching it lightly. Work down the sides, then press the bottom strip onto the tape. The film will be slightly loose at this stage.

Run the hairdryer on high heat across the film surface in slow sweeping passes, holding the dryer 50-100 mm from the film. The film contracts and pulls taut as it warms. Work from the centre outward. Wrinkles disappear as the film shrinks.
Trim excess film around the perimeter with a stanley knife against a straight edge once the film is taut and cool.

Peel the tape and film away together from one corner. Kit tape is designed to release without taking paint with it. If the tape has been left more than one full year, heat the tape strip with the hairdryer before pulling to soften the adhesive and reduce the risk of paint damage.

The window cannot be opened while the film is in place. This rules it out for windows needed for ventilation. It is not a replacement for draught-proofing: the film reduces conductive and radiative heat loss through the glass but does not seal the frame gaps through which cold air infiltrates. Combine film with pile-strip draught-proofing on the sash or casement for the full benefit.
